questions.stem: A 45-year-old asymptomatic man presents for a routine health check. His blood pressure is 152/96 mmHg on two separate occasions. He is a non-smoker, drinks minimal alcohol, and has no family history of hypertension. Examination is unremarkable. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The patient has persistently elevated clinic BP (≥140/90 on two occasions) with no identifiable cause, making essential hypertension the most likely diagnosis. White-coat hypertension would require normal ambulatory BP, which has not been assessed. Secondary causes are less common without clinical clues.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with no significant past medical history is found to have BP 162/98 mmHg during a pharmacy check. She feels well, has no headaches, palpitations or flushes. Her BMI is 28, and she takes no medications. Urinalysis and renal function are normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The patient has stage 2 hypertension with no secondary features, so essential hypertension is most likely. Isolated systolic hypertension would require diastolic <90 mmHg. White-coat hypertension is possible but less likely than sustained hypertension at this age and level. Phaeochromocytoma is rare without paroxysmal symptoms.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with no known medical problems presents to the Emergency Department with a severe headache and blurred vision. His BP is 230/140 mmHg. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilloedema and flame-shaped haemorrhages. Urinalysis shows 1+ protein. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of papilloedema, haemorrhages, and very high BP with end-organ effects (retinopathy, proteinuria) defines accelerated-malignant hypertension (hypertensive emergency). Hypertensive urgency lacks acute target organ damage. Stroke or migraine would not explain all features.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of untreated hypertension presents with sudden onset severe tearing chest pain radiating to his back. His BP is 210/120 mmHg in the right arm and 170/90 mmHg in the left arm. Chest X-ray shows a widened mediastinum. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The tearing chest pain with unequal arm BPs and widened mediastinum is classic for aortic dissection, a life-threatening complication of long-standing hypertension. Myocardial infarction pain is typically central and not radiating to the back with pulse deficits. Hypertensive emergency does not directly explain the dissection.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks gestation is noted to have BP 150/95 mmHg at a routine antenatal check. She has no headaches or visual symptoms. Urinalysis shows 2+ protein. Her booking BP at 12 weeks was 115/75 mmHg. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: New-onset hypertension after 20 weeks with proteinuria (≥1+) in a previously normotensive woman defines pre-eclampsia. Essential or chronic hypertension would predate pregnancy or present earlier. Gestational hypertension lacks proteinuria. White-coat is excluded by elevated BP at clinic.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man has a blood pressure of 158/94 mmHg at a routine clinic check. He has no symptoms, no comorbidities, and his urine dipstick is negative. What is the most appropriate next step to confirm a diagnosis of hypertension?
options: Repeat clinic blood pressure in one month
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend confirming a new diagnosis of hypertension with 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) if clinic readings are ≥140/90 mmHg. Home blood pressure monitoring is an alternative if ABPM is not tolerated, but ABPM is the preferred first test. The other options are not first-line for diagnosis; urine catecholamines and renal artery duplex are reserved for suspected secondary hypertension.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with newly diagnosed stage 1 hypertension (clinic BP 145/90 mmHg) has no target organ damage on examination. Which single investigation is most appropriate to perform before initiating antihypertensive therapy?
questions.explanation: Baseline renal function (serum creatinine and eGFR) is essential to assess for chronic kidney disease and to guide choice of antihypertensive therapy. While urine ACR, ECG, and fundoscopy are also recommended for target organ damage assessment, renal function is the most critical baseline investigation before starting treatment.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with severe headache and a blood pressure of 220/130 mmHg. Fundoscopy reveals papilloedema. Which single investigation is most appropriate to identify a secondary cause of her hypertension?
questions.explanation: In a young patient with severe hypertension and papilloedema (hypertensive emergency), phaeochromocytoma is a 'never-miss' cause. Urine metanephrines (or plasma metanephrines) have high sensitivity and specificity for diagnosis. Renal artery stenosis, hyperaldosteronism, and drug-induced hypertension are also possible but less likely given the severity and age.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with long-standing hypertension has an ECG showing left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) by voltage criteria. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the presence of hypertensive heart disease?
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography is the standard first-line imaging to confirm left ventricular hypertrophy and assess diastolic function in hypertensive heart disease. Cardiac MRI is more accurate but reserved for cases where echocardiography is inadequate. The other options are not confirmatory for LVH.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with known essential hypertension has a 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitor. The report shows: average daytime BP 148/92 mmHg, average night-time BP 140/85 mmHg. Which of the following best describes her nocturnal blood pressure pattern?
questions.explanation: A non-dipper pattern is defined as a nocturnal blood pressure fall of less than 10% compared to daytime. Here, systolic drops from 148 to 140 mmHg (5.4% drop), confirming a non-dipper pattern. Dipper would require >10% drop; reverse dipper involves a rise; extreme dipper >20% drop; masked hypertension refers to normal clinic but elevated out-of-office BP.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old white man is diagnosed with essential hypertension (clinic BP 152/96 mmHg). He has no other medical conditions and is not taking any medication. What is the most appropriate first-line antihypertensive monotherapy?
options: Thiazide diuretic (e.g., bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg once daily)
Calcium channel blocker (e.g., amlodipine 5 mg once daily)
ACE inhibitor (e.g., ramipril 2.5 mg once daily)
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend an ACE inhibitor (or ARB) as first-line monotherapy for non-black patients under 55 years. Beta-blockers are no longer first-line due to inferior cardiovascular outcomes. CCBs or thiazides may be considered if ACEi is not tolerated.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old black woman of African-Caribbean descent is newly diagnosed with hypertension (clinic BP 148/90 mmHg). She has no other comorbidities. What is the most appropriate first-line antihypertensive?
options: Angiotensin receptor blocker (e.g., losartan 50 mg once daily)
Calcium channel blocker (e.g., amlodipine 5 mg once daily)
Thiazide diuretic (e.g., indapamide 2.5 mg once daily)
ACE inhibitor (e.g., lisinopril 10 mg once daily)
questions.explanation: NICE recommends a calcium channel blocker as first-line for black patients (any age) because they tend to have lower renin levels and respond better to CCBs than to ACEi/ARBs. Thiazides are second-line, and beta-blockers are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known asthma presents with hypertension (clinic BP 155/92 mmHg). He is not on any other medications. Which antihypertensive class is CONTRAINDICATED?
questions.explanation: Beta-blockers are contraindicated in asthma because they can cause bronchospasm via blockade of beta-2 receptors. ACE inhibitors may cause cough but are not contraindicated; CCBs, thiazides, and alpha-blockers are safe.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 35%) and hypertension (clinic BP 158/94 mmHg) is being started on antihypertensive therapy. He is currently not on any antihypertensives. What is the most appropriate first-line agent?
options: Thiazide diuretic (e.g., bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg once daily)
Calcium channel blocker (e.g., amlodipine 5 mg once daily)
ACE inhibitor (e.g., ramipril 2.5 mg once daily)
questions.explanation: In heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, ACE inhibitors (or ARBs) are the first-line antihypertensives due to mortality benefit. Beta-blockers are also indicated but typically added after ACEi. CCBs (non-dihydropyridine) may be avoided for negative inotropic effects; thiazides and alpha-blockers are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with resistant hypertension is already taking amlodipine 10 mg, ramipril 10 mg, and indapamide 2.5 mg daily. Her clinic BP remains 158/98 mmHg. She has no contraindications. What is the most appropriate fourth-line add-on therapy?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends spironolactone (or an aldosterone antagonist) as fourth-line therapy for resistant hypertension. Doxazosin is an alternative but not first-line; beta-blockers are not typically added at this stage; moxonidine and hydralazine are later options.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man attends his GP for a routine health check. His blood pressure is 158/96 mmHg, confirmed on two further readings during the same visit. He is asymptomatic, has no comorbidities, and reports no family history of hypertension. Urinalysis and baseline renal function are normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Advise lifestyle modifications and repeat BP in 6 weeks
Refer to nephrology for further investigation
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend confirming a diagnosis of hypertension with ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) or home blood pressure monitoring before initiating treatment. A single clinic reading of 158/96 mmHg is insufficient to diagnose hypertension. Lifestyle advice is important but should accompany confirmation, not replace it.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with a history of essential hypertension is reviewed in clinic. She has been taking amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily for 6 months. Today her blood pressure is 142/88 mmHg. She reports no side effects and her renal function is normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Increase amlodipine to 10 mg once daily
Repeat blood pressure measurement in 3 months
questions.explanation: According to NICE, if blood pressure is not controlled on a single agent, the dose should be increased to the maximum recommended before adding a second drug. Amlodipine 5 mg is a starting dose; increasing to 10 mg is the next logical step. Adding a second agent is premature.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe headache, confusion, and blurred vision. His blood pressure is 220/130 mmHg. Fundoscopy reveals papilledema. He has no previous medical history. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a CT scan of the head
questions.explanation: This is a hypertensive emergency with evidence of end-organ damage (hypertensive encephalopathy, papilledema). Immediate blood pressure reduction is required to prevent further damage. Intravenous labetalol is recommended for controlled reduction. CT head may be considered after initiating treatment but is not the first step. Oral nifedipine can cause rapid, uncontrolled drops in pressure and should be avoided.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with essential hypertension and stage 3 chronic kidney disease (eGFR 45 mL/min/1.73m²) is on ramipril 10 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily. His blood pressure remains 165/100 mmHg. He has no symptoms and his serum potassium is 4.5 mmol/L. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: NICE step 3 for uncontrolled hypertension on maximal A+C (ACEi/ARB + calcium channel blocker) is to add a thiazide-like diuretic, such as bendroflumethiazide. Spironolactone is step 4 but with caution in CKD due to hyperkalaemia. Specialist referral is not the next step before adding a third agent.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with known essential hypertension controlled on ramipril 10 mg daily presents to the antenatal clinic at 16 weeks gestation. She stopped her contraception 3 months ago and only recently discovered she is pregnant. Her blood pressure today is 135/85 mmHg. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Stop ramipril and start methyldopa 250 mg twice daily
Stop ramipril and advise no antihypertensive therapy as BP is well controlled
Stop ramipril and start labetalol 100 mg twice daily
Refer to a cardiologist for advice
questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors (e.g., ramipril) are contraindicated in pregnancy due to risk of fetal harm. For chronic hypertension in pregnancy, NICE recommends methyldopa, labetalol, or nifedipine. Methyldopa is often first-line due to its long-established safety profile. Labetalol is also suitable, but methyldopa is the preferred first-line option in many guidelines.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old male with no significant history presents for a routine check. He feels well. His blood pressure is 185/115 mmHg on two separate occasions. Which single clinical finding would confirm that this is a hypertensive emergency?
questions.explanation: Hypertensive emergency requires evidence of acute end-organ damage. Papilledema indicates acute hypertensive retinopathy (grade IV) and confirms the emergency. Blood pressure >180/120 mmHg alone is a hypertensive urgency, not an emergency, if no end-organ damage is present.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old male with hypertension is on amlodipine 10 mg, losartan 50 mg, and chlorthalidone 25 mg. His clinic blood pressure is 158/94 mmHg, and home readings average 145/88 mmHg. Which finding should prompt investigation for secondary hypertension?
options: Symptoms suggestive of obstructive sleep apnoea
questions.explanation: Hypokalaemia in a patient with resistant hypertension is a key clue to primary hyperaldosteronism, a common secondary cause. Elevated creatinine, mild proteinuria, sleep apnoea, and obesity are less specific and do not alone warrant immediate secondary screening.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old male with known hypertension presents to the emergency department with sudden severe tearing chest pain radiating to the back. Right arm blood pressure is 200/120 mmHg, left arm 160/85 mmHg. Which clinical finding most strongly suggests aortic dissection?
options: Difference in blood pressure between arms >20 mmHg
questions.explanation: A difference in blood pressure between arms of >20 mmHg is a specific sign of aortic dissection, indicating involvement of the arch vessels. While chest pain is classic, the BP differential is more specific and often an early clinical clue.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old female with long-standing hypertension presents with acute dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and bilateral crackles on lung auscultation. Blood pressure is 190/110 mmHg. Which examination finding is most indicative that hypertensive heart disease is the underlying cause?
options: Loud pulmonary component of second heart sound (P2)
questions.explanation: A displaced apex beat suggests left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH), a chronic consequence of hypertension and hallmark of hypertensive heart disease. While other signs indicate heart failure, LVH points specifically to the aetiology.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 30 weeks gestation presents with a blood pressure of 160/100 mmHg and proteinuria 2+ on dipstick. Which clinical finding indicates severe pre-eclampsia requiring urgent delivery?
questions.explanation: Epigastric pain in pre-eclampsia is a red flag for hepatic capsule distension and possible HELLP syndrome, indicating severe disease often requiring urgent delivery. While all options can occur, epigastric pain is particularly concerning for life-threatening complications.
